questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The process by which sensory receptors convert physical energy into neural signals is called:

choices.text: Perception
choices.explanation: Perception is the brain's interpretation of sensory information — the conversion step is a separate, earlier process.

choices.text: Transduction
choices.explanation: Transduction is the conversion of physical energy (light, sound, pressure, chemicals) into neural impulses that the nervous system can process.

choices.text: Adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation is the diminished sensitivity to an unchanging stimulus — it is not the initial conversion of energy into neural signals.

choices.text: Accommodation
choices.explanation: Accommodation is the process by which the lens changes shape to focus light on the retina — it is specific to vision, not the general energy-to-signal conversion.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The absolute threshold is defined as the minimum stimulation needed to detect a stimulus:

choices.text: 100% of the time
choices.explanation: Detection 100% of the time would require a stimulus well above threshold — the absolute threshold is defined at a lower detection rate.

choices.text: 50% of the time
choices.explanation: The absolute threshold is the minimum stimulation needed to detect a stimulus 50% of the time — this accounts for the fact that detection near threshold is probabilistic.

choices.text: 75% of the time
choices.explanation: The standard definition uses 50%, not 75% — 75% detection would require a stimulus above the absolute threshold.

choices.text: 25% of the time
choices.explanation: Detection 25% of the time would describe a stimulus below the absolute threshold, not at it.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which retinal receptors are responsible for color vision and sharp detail in bright light?

choices.text: Rods
choices.explanation: Rods are sensitive to dim light and peripheral vision but do not detect color or fine detail.

choices.text: Cones
choices.explanation: Cones are concentrated in the fovea and are responsible for detecting color and fine detail, but they require bright light to function.

choices.text: Bipolar cells
choices.explanation: Bipolar cells are intermediate neurons in the retina that connect rods and cones to ganglion cells — they are not the primary photoreceptors.

choices.text: Ganglion cells
choices.explanation: Ganglion cells transmit visual information from the retina to the brain via the optic nerve — they are not photoreceptors.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The pitch of a sound is determined by the _____ of the sound wave.

choices.text: Amplitude
choices.explanation: Amplitude determines the loudness (volume) of a sound, not its pitch.

choices.text: Frequency
choices.explanation: Pitch is determined by the frequency (number of wave cycles per second) of a sound wave — higher frequency produces higher pitch.

choices.text: Purity
choices.explanation: Purity (complexity) of a sound wave determines timbre — the quality that distinguishes different instruments playing the same note.

choices.text: Duration
choices.explanation: Duration determines how long a sound lasts, not its perceived pitch.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Gestalt principle of closure refers to the tendency to:

choices.text: Group nearby objects together
choices.explanation: Grouping nearby objects together is the Gestalt principle of proximity, not closure.

choices.text: Fill in gaps to perceive incomplete figures as complete
choices.explanation: Closure is the brain's tendency to fill in missing information to perceive incomplete patterns as whole, complete objects.

choices.text: Perceive smooth, continuous lines rather than jagged ones
choices.explanation: Perceiving smooth, continuous patterns is the Gestalt principle of continuity, not closure.

choices.text: Group similar-looking objects together
choices.explanation: Grouping similar objects together is the Gestalt principle of similarity, not closure.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Sensory adaptation refers to:

choices.text: The ability to detect stimuli below the conscious threshold
choices.explanation: Detecting stimuli below conscious awareness relates to subliminal perception, not sensory adaptation.

choices.text: Reduced sensitivity to an unchanging stimulus over time
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation occurs when constant stimulation causes receptors to become less responsive — for example, you stop noticing a constant smell after a few minutes.

choices.text: The brain's interpretation of sensory input as meaningful
choices.explanation: Interpreting sensory input is perception — sensory adaptation is about decreased responsiveness to constant stimuli.

choices.text: Increased sensitivity to stimuli in a new environment
choices.explanation: Increased sensitivity would be sensitization — adaptation is the opposite, a decrease in responsiveness.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The cochlea is located in the:

choices.text: Outer ear
choices.explanation: The outer ear includes the pinna and ear canal — the cochlea is deeper inside the ear.

choices.text: Middle ear
choices.explanation: The middle ear contains the three ossicles (hammer, anvil, stirrup) — the cochlea is in the next section inward.

choices.text: Inner ear
choices.explanation: The cochlea is a fluid-filled, spiral-shaped structure in the inner ear where hair cells transduce sound vibrations into neural signals.

choices.text: Temporal lobe
choices.explanation: The temporal lobe is a brain region that processes auditory information — the cochlea is a physical structure in the ear, not in the brain.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Top-down processing relies most heavily on:

choices.text: Raw sensory data from the environment
choices.explanation: Relying on raw sensory data describes bottom-up processing, which builds perception from the stimulus itself.

choices.text: Prior knowledge, expectations, and context
choices.explanation: Top-down processing uses existing knowledge, expectations, and context to interpret sensory input — it works from the 'top' (cognition) downward to shape perception.

choices.text: The intensity of the physical stimulus
choices.explanation: Stimulus intensity affects sensation, not top-down processing — top-down processing is about cognitive influence on perception.

choices.text: The number of sensory receptors activated
choices.explanation: Receptor activation is part of the sensory (bottom-up) process — top-down processing operates at the cognitive level.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The iris controls the amount of light entering the eye by adjusting the size of the:

choices.text: Lens
choices.explanation: The lens focuses light on the retina by changing shape (accommodation) — it does not control the amount of light entering the eye.

choices.text: Cornea
choices.explanation: The cornea is a transparent covering that bends light as it enters the eye — it does not change size to regulate light.

choices.text: Pupil
choices.explanation: The iris is a ring of muscle that dilates or constricts the pupil — the adjustable opening that controls how much light enters the eye.

choices.text: Retina
choices.explanation: The retina is the light-sensitive surface at the back of the eye — it does not change size to regulate incoming light.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The gate-control theory is most relevant to the perception of:

choices.text: Color
choices.explanation: Color perception is explained by trichromatic and opponent-process theories, not gate-control theory.

choices.text: Pain
choices.explanation: Gate-control theory proposes that the spinal cord contains a neural 'gate' that can be opened or closed to allow or block pain signals from reaching the brain.

choices.text: Pitch
choices.explanation: Pitch perception is explained by place theory and frequency theory, not gate-control theory.

choices.text: Depth
choices.explanation: Depth perception is explained by binocular and monocular cues — gate-control theory specifically addresses pain.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A psychologist places a 1 kg weight in a participant's hand and gradually adds weight until the participant notices a difference. The participant first notices the change when the weight reaches 1.02 kg. The psychologist then repeats the experiment starting with a 5 kg weight.

questions.question: According to Weber's Law, the participant holding the 5 kg weight will first notice an increase when the weight reaches approximately:

choices.explanation: Weber's Law says the JND is a constant proportion (2% = 0.02/1), not a constant amount — so the JND for 5 kg is 5 × 0.02 = 0.10 kg, not 0.02 kg.
Weber's Law: JND is a constant proportion of the original stimulus. The ratio is 0.02/1.00 = 2%. For 5 kg: 5.00 × 0.02 = 0.10 kg. The participant will notice at approximately 5.10 kg.
A 0.50 kg increase would be a 10% change, far exceeding the 2% Weber fraction established in the first trial.
A 2 kg increase would be a 40% change — far more than needed. Weber's Law predicts a proportionally smaller increase of just 2%.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Signal detection theory differs from classical threshold theory in that signal detection theory accounts for:

choices.text: The physical intensity of stimuli
choices.explanation: Both classical and signal detection theories consider stimulus intensity — this is not the distinguishing factor.

choices.text: Individual decision criteria such as motivation and expectations
choices.explanation: Signal detection theory adds psychological factors (motivation, expectations, fatigue, potential consequences) to the physical sensitivity component, explaining why the same person may detect the same stimulus differently depending on context.

choices.text: The speed of neural transmission
choices.explanation: Neural transmission speed is a biological factor that neither theory specifically addresses as its distinguishing feature.

choices.text: Differences between the five senses
choices.explanation: Signal detection theory applies to all senses equally — its key contribution is accounting for response bias, not sense-specific differences.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The trichromatic theory best explains color processing at the level of the _____, while the opponent-process theory best explains processing at the level of the _____.

choices.text: Brain; retina
choices.explanation: This reverses the correct order — trichromatic theory operates at the retinal level (three cone types) and opponent-process at the neural/brain level.

choices.text: Retina; brain and ganglion cells
choices.explanation: Trichromatic theory explains how three types of retinal cones detect color, while opponent-process theory explains how ganglion cells and brain neurons process color in opposing pairs — both theories are correct at different levels.

choices.text: Optic nerve; lens
choices.explanation: Neither theory operates at the lens level — the lens focuses light and has no role in color processing.

choices.text: Cornea; iris
choices.explanation: The cornea and iris are structures involved in light entry, not color processing — color detection begins at the retinal receptor level.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: After staring at a green square for 30 seconds, a participant looks at a white surface and reports seeing a red square.

questions.question: This afterimage effect is best explained by:

choices.text: Trichromatic theory
choices.explanation: Trichromatic theory explains how three cone types detect different wavelengths — it does not explain why viewing one color produces its complementary color as an afterimage.

choices.text: Opponent-process theory
choices.explanation: Opponent-process theory explains afterimages: staring at green fatigues the green response in the red-green opponent channel. When looking at white, the red response rebounds unopposed, creating a red afterimage.

choices.text: Place theory
choices.explanation: Place theory explains pitch perception in hearing — it has nothing to do with color vision or afterimages.

choices.text: Gate-control theory
choices.explanation: Gate-control theory explains pain perception — it is unrelated to color vision phenomena.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Place theory best explains our perception of _____ sounds, while frequency theory best explains our perception of _____ sounds.

choices.text: Low-pitched; high-pitched
choices.explanation: This is reversed — frequency theory handles low-pitched sounds (neurons can fire in sync with slow waves) and place theory handles high-pitched sounds.

choices.text: High-pitched; low-pitched
choices.explanation: Place theory explains high-pitched sounds (different frequencies activate specific locations on the basilar membrane), while frequency theory explains low-pitched sounds (neurons fire at the rate of the sound wave). The volley principle bridges mid-range frequencies.

choices.text: Loud; soft
choices.explanation: Place and frequency theories explain pitch (frequency) perception, not loudness (amplitude) perception.

choices.text: Near; far
choices.explanation: These theories explain pitch perception, not sound localization — distance perception involves different auditory cues.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A person with sensorineural hearing loss would benefit least from:

choices.text: A cochlear implant
choices.explanation: Cochlear implants bypass damaged hair cells and directly stimulate the auditory nerve — they are specifically designed for sensorineural hearing loss.

choices.text: A hearing aid that amplifies sound
choices.explanation: Hearing aids amplify sound, which helps with conductive hearing loss. In sensorineural loss, the hair cells or auditory nerve are damaged — making sounds louder does not help if the transduction mechanism itself is broken.

choices.text: Sign language training
choices.explanation: Sign language is a viable communication alternative for severe sensorineural hearing loss — it does not depend on hearing at all.

choices.text: Visual communication aids
choices.explanation: Visual aids can help compensate for hearing loss of any type — they are not specific to conductive loss.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A radiologist is examining X-rays for signs of a rare tumor that appears in only 1 out of every 1,000 scans. Despite being well-trained, the radiologist occasionally misses the tumor when it is present.

questions.question: Signal detection theory would predict that the radiologist's miss rate is influenced by:

choices.text: Only the physical clarity of the X-ray image
choices.explanation: Signal detection theory states that detection depends on both physical sensitivity and psychological factors — image clarity alone is insufficient.

choices.text: The low base rate of the tumor and the radiologist's response criterion
choices.explanation: When a target is very rare (low base rate), the observer's response criterion shifts — they become less likely to report a detection because 'no tumor' is almost always correct. Fatigue, expectations, and costs of false alarms also influence the criterion.

choices.text: The size of the radiologist's blind spot
choices.explanation: The blind spot is a feature of normal eye anatomy — radiologists are trained to scan systematically, and the blind spot is not the explanation for detection failures in this context.

choices.text: Weber's Law and the difference threshold
choices.explanation: Weber's Law describes the ability to detect differences between two stimuli — the radiologist's task is detecting a signal (tumor) in noise, which is signal detection, not difference threshold.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Retinal disparity is a binocular cue for depth perception that relies on:

choices.text: The difference in images received by each eye due to their horizontal separation
choices.explanation: Because our eyes are about 2.5 inches apart, each receives a slightly different image — the brain computes depth from these differences. Greater disparity indicates closer objects.

choices.text: The degree to which the eyes turn inward to focus on a near object
choices.explanation: This describes convergence, another binocular cue — retinal disparity specifically refers to the difference between the two retinal images, not eye muscle feedback.

choices.text: One object partially blocking another
choices.explanation: This is interposition (overlap), a monocular cue — it requires only one eye and is not based on the difference between two retinal images.

choices.text: Distant objects appearing hazy compared to near objects
choices.explanation: Atmospheric haze affecting distant objects is the monocular cue of aerial perspective — it is not related to binocular retinal disparity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A person sees a familiar friend from far away and perceives them as normal-sized even though the retinal image is very small. This demonstrates:

choices.text: Size constancy
choices.explanation: Size constancy is the perceptual tendency to see an object as the same size regardless of distance — the brain uses distance cues to adjust the perceived size despite changes in the retinal image.

choices.text: Shape constancy
choices.explanation: Shape constancy is perceiving an object as the same shape regardless of viewing angle (e.g., a door looks rectangular whether open or closed) — this scenario involves size, not shape.

choices.text: Sensory adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation is decreased sensitivity to a constant stimulus — this scenario involves perceiving a distant object at its actual size, which is a constancy, not adaptation.

choices.text: Figure-ground perception
choices.explanation: Figure-ground involves distinguishing an object from its background — perceiving correct size at a distance is a different perceptual process.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following is a monocular depth cue?

choices.text: Retinal disparity
choices.explanation: Retinal disparity requires both eyes to compare slightly different images — it is a binocular cue.

choices.text: Convergence
choices.explanation: Convergence involves both eyes turning inward to focus on near objects — it is a binocular cue.

choices.text: Linear perspective
choices.explanation: Linear perspective — parallel lines appearing to converge in the distance — can be perceived with just one eye, making it a monocular depth cue.

choices.text: Stereopsis
choices.explanation: Stereopsis is the perception of depth from retinal disparity, requiring both eyes — it is a binocular process.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A psychology student reads a sentence with a missing word: 'The old man the boats.' Initially, the student reads 'old man' as a noun phrase and is confused. Upon re-reading, she realizes 'old' is a noun (elderly people) and 'man' is a verb (operate).

questions.question: The student's initial confusion best illustrates the influence of:

choices.text: Bottom-up processing
choices.explanation: Bottom-up processing would involve parsing each word individually — the confusion arose because the student's expectations (top-down) led her to misinterpret the grammar.

choices.text: Perceptual set
choices.explanation: The student's prior experience with common English phrases created a perceptual set — a predisposition to interpret 'old man' as an adjective-noun pair, causing initial misreading of the sentence's actual structure.

choices.text: Sensory adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation involves decreased sensitivity to constant stimulation — this scenario is about cognitive misinterpretation, not sensory responsiveness.

choices.text: The absolute threshold for text
choices.explanation: The student could clearly see the text — the issue was interpretation (perception), not detection (sensation).

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The kinesthetic sense provides information about:

choices.text: The position and movement of body parts
choices.explanation: Kinesthesis (proprioception) uses receptors in muscles, tendons, and joints to provide awareness of body position and movement without visual feedback.

choices.text: Balance and head orientation in space
choices.explanation: Balance and head position are provided by the vestibular sense (semicircular canals), not the kinesthetic sense.

choices.text: Pain intensity and location
choices.explanation: Pain perception involves nociceptors and the gate-control mechanism — the kinesthetic sense provides position and movement information, not pain.

choices.text: Temperature changes on the skin
choices.explanation: Temperature detection is part of the somatosensory system (thermoreceptors in the skin) — kinesthesis specifically tracks body position and movement.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Unlike all other senses, olfactory information is processed without first passing through the:

choices.text: Cerebral cortex
choices.explanation: Olfactory information does reach the cerebral cortex — but it takes a unique route that bypasses another structure first.

choices.text: Thalamus
choices.explanation: Smell is the only sense that does not route through the thalamus. Olfactory signals go directly from the olfactory bulb to the cortex and limbic system, which explains why smells can trigger strong emotional memories.

choices.text: Limbic system
choices.explanation: Olfactory information actually goes directly to the limbic system (especially the amygdala) — smell is closely connected to emotion and memory.

choices.text: Brain stem
choices.explanation: While smell bypasses the thalamus, olfactory pathways do connect with brainstem regions — the thalamus bypass is the unique feature of olfaction.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A researcher shows participants an ambiguous image that could be seen as either a young woman or an old woman. Before viewing, half the participants are shown pictures of young women, and the other half are shown pictures of elderly women.

questions.question: Participants who first saw pictures of young women will most likely perceive the ambiguous image as a young woman. This demonstrates:

choices.text: Bottom-up processing
choices.explanation: Bottom-up processing builds perception from the raw stimulus — the prior exposure to young women's pictures is a top-down influence shaping interpretation.

choices.text: Top-down processing influenced by perceptual set
choices.explanation: The prior exposure created a perceptual set — a mental predisposition to interpret ambiguous stimuli in a particular way. This is top-down processing because prior experience (not the stimulus itself) drives the interpretation.

choices.text: Sensory adaptation to faces
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation involves decreased sensitivity to constant stimuli — viewing different images beforehand is a priming effect, not adaptation.

choices.text: The absolute threshold for face recognition
choices.explanation: The absolute threshold concerns minimum detection — this study is about interpretation of a clearly visible but ambiguous image.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The fovea is the area of the retina where:

choices.text: Rods are most densely concentrated for night vision
choices.explanation: Rods are concentrated in the peripheral retina — the fovea contains almost no rods.

choices.text: The optic nerve exits the eye, creating a blind spot
choices.explanation: The blind spot is where the optic nerve exits — the fovea is a different structure at the center of the retina.

choices.text: Cones are most densely concentrated for sharp, detailed vision
choices.explanation: The fovea is the central point of the retina with the highest concentration of cones, providing the sharpest, most detailed color vision — this is where you focus when reading or examining details.

choices.text: Light first enters and is refracted toward the lens
choices.explanation: Light enters through the cornea at the front of the eye — the fovea is at the back of the eye on the retina.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A soldier in combat does not notice a painful wound until after the battle ends. This is best explained by:

choices.text: Weber's Law
choices.explanation: Weber's Law describes the relationship between stimulus magnitude and just noticeable differences — it does not explain blocked pain awareness during intense situations.

choices.text: Gate-control theory
choices.explanation: Gate-control theory explains how the brain can close the spinal 'gate' to pain signals during intense stress or distraction — competing signals from the battle situation block pain transmission to the brain.

choices.text: Sensory adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation involves gradual decrease in sensitivity to constant stimulation — pain from a wound does not follow this pattern, and the soldier simply did not notice the pain during combat.

choices.text: Trichromatic theory
choices.explanation: Trichromatic theory explains color vision — it has no relevance to pain perception.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The vestibular sense detects:

choices.text: Touch, pressure, and temperature on the skin
choices.explanation: Touch, pressure, and temperature are detected by the somatosensory system's skin receptors, not the vestibular sense.

choices.text: Body position and movement of limbs
choices.explanation: Body position and limb movement are detected by kinesthesis (proprioception) using receptors in muscles and joints, not the vestibular sense.

choices.text: Head position, balance, and acceleration
choices.explanation: The vestibular sense uses the semicircular canals and vestibular sacs in the inner ear to detect head position, rotational movement, and linear acceleration — crucial for maintaining balance.

choices.text: Chemical substances dissolved in saliva
choices.explanation: Detecting chemicals in saliva is the function of gustatory (taste) receptors on the tongue, not the vestibular sense.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: In vision, the process of accommodation involves:

choices.text: The pupil dilating to let in more light
choices.explanation: Pupil dilation is controlled by the iris to regulate light — accommodation specifically involves the lens changing shape.

choices.text: The lens changing shape to focus light on the retina
choices.explanation: Accommodation is the process by which ciliary muscles change the curvature of the lens — flattening it for distant objects and thickening it for near objects — to keep images focused sharply on the retina.

choices.text: The retina adjusting its sensitivity in different lighting conditions
choices.explanation: Retinal sensitivity adjustment is called light/dark adaptation — accommodation specifically refers to the lens changing shape for focus.

choices.text: Both eyes converging to focus on a near object
choices.explanation: Both eyes turning inward is convergence, a binocular depth cue — accommodation is a monocular process involving the lens.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Texture gradient is a monocular depth cue based on the principle that:

choices.text: Closer objects appear higher in the visual field
choices.explanation: Objects appearing higher in the visual field is the monocular cue of relative height — texture gradient involves surface detail.

choices.text: Surface details become finer and less distinct with increasing distance
choices.explanation: Texture gradient provides depth information because the texture of a surface (like grass, tiles, or gravel) appears increasingly compressed and less detailed as the surface recedes into the distance.

choices.text: Moving objects appear to travel faster when closer
choices.explanation: This describes motion parallax — the relative apparent motion of objects at different distances — not texture gradient.

choices.text: Shadows fall on one side of objects to indicate three-dimensionality
choices.explanation: Shadow-based depth perception is the monocular cue of light and shadow (chiaroscuro), not texture gradient.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Gestalt principle of proximity predicts that people will:

choices.text: Fill in gaps to see incomplete shapes as whole
choices.explanation: Filling in gaps to see whole shapes is the Gestalt principle of closure, not proximity.

choices.text: Perceive objects that are close together as belonging to the same group
choices.explanation: Proximity states that objects near each other tend to be grouped together — for example, three dots close together and three dots farther away are seen as two separate groups.

choices.text: See continuous smooth lines rather than abrupt direction changes
choices.explanation: Perceiving smooth, continuous patterns is the Gestalt principle of continuity, not proximity.

choices.text: Distinguish a main object from its surrounding background
choices.explanation: Separating the main object from background is the Gestalt principle of figure-ground, not proximity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The wavelength of light primarily determines our perception of:

choices.text: Brightness
choices.explanation: Brightness is determined by the intensity (amplitude) of light, not its wavelength.

choices.text: Color
choices.explanation: Wavelength determines the hue (color) we perceive — short wavelengths appear blue/violet, medium wavelengths appear green, and long wavelengths appear red.

choices.text: Depth
choices.explanation: Depth perception involves binocular and monocular cues — it is not determined by the wavelength of light.

choices.text: Size
choices.explanation: Size perception depends on retinal image size combined with distance cues — wavelength does not determine perceived size.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Motion parallax provides depth information because:

choices.text: Closer objects appear to move faster than distant objects when the observer moves
choices.explanation: When you move your head or travel in a vehicle, nearby objects appear to rush past while distant objects appear to move slowly or remain stationary — this relative motion provides monocular depth information.

choices.text: Each eye receives a slightly different image of the same scene
choices.explanation: Different images from each eye describes retinal disparity, a binocular cue — motion parallax is monocular and depends on observer movement.

choices.text: Objects reflect more light when they are closer to the observer
choices.explanation: Light reflection intensity is not the basis of motion parallax — it depends on the apparent speed of objects at different distances during movement.

choices.text: Parallel lines converge at a vanishing point in the distance
choices.explanation: This describes linear perspective — a different monocular cue that involves line convergence, not apparent motion.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: Researchers fitted kittens with goggles that only allowed them to see vertical lines during a critical period of visual development. After the goggles were removed, the kittens had difficulty perceiving horizontal lines even though their eyes were physically normal.

questions.question: This study best demonstrates that:

choices.text: The retina requires exposure to all orientations to develop properly
choices.explanation: The kittens' retinas and eyes were physically normal — the deficit was in cortical processing, not retinal development.

choices.text: Perceptual abilities require specific environmental input during critical developmental periods
choices.explanation: The visual cortex requires exposure to various orientations during critical periods to develop the neural connections needed to process them — without early exposure to horizontal lines, the relevant cortical neurons did not develop properly.

choices.text: Cats are incapable of perceiving horizontal lines due to evolutionary adaptation
choices.explanation: Cats normally perceive all orientations — this deficit was artificially created by restricting visual experience during development.

choices.text: Sensory adaptation permanently alters visual perception
choices.explanation: This is not sensory adaptation (temporary decreased sensitivity) — it is a permanent developmental change due to restricted experience during a critical period.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A person who is red-green colorblind most likely has a deficiency in:

choices.text: Rod photoreceptors in the retina
choices.explanation: Rods do not detect color — they only provide dim-light vision. Color blindness involves cone deficiencies.

choices.text: One or more types of cone photoreceptors
choices.explanation: Red-green color blindness results from missing or defective red (long-wavelength) or green (medium-wavelength) cone photoreceptors, preventing the person from distinguishing between these colors — supporting the trichromatic theory.

choices.text: The opponent-process cells in the optic nerve
choices.explanation: While opponent-process theory explains some color vision phenomena, red-green colorblindness is caused by cone receptor deficiencies at the retinal level, not opponent-process cell damage.

choices.text: The visual cortex in the occipital lobe
choices.explanation: Red-green colorblindness is a retinal receptor problem, not a cortical processing deficit — the visual cortex typically functions normally in colorblind individuals.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: In a study on inattentional blindness, participants were asked to count the number of passes made by a basketball team in a video. A person in a gorilla suit walked through the scene for several seconds. Approximately 50% of participants failed to notice the gorilla.

questions.question: This finding challenges the assumption that:

choices.text: Vision requires light to enter the eye
choices.explanation: The gorilla was clearly visible — the issue was not about physical sensation but about perceptual attention.

choices.text: We consciously perceive all stimuli that fall on our retinas
choices.explanation: Inattentional blindness demonstrates that focused attention can cause us to completely miss unexpected but clearly visible stimuli — perception requires attention, not just retinal stimulation.

choices.text: The absolute threshold for visual detection is the same for all people
choices.explanation: The gorilla was well above the absolute threshold for all participants — the failure was attentional, not sensory.

choices.text: Bottom-up processing is less reliable than top-down processing
choices.explanation: The finding demonstrates the power of top-down attentional focus to override bottom-up stimulus detection — it does not claim bottom-up processing is generally unreliable.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Müller-Lyer illusion, in which two lines of equal length appear different due to inward or outward-pointing arrows, is best explained by:

choices.text: Differences in retinal image size
choices.explanation: The retinal images of the two lines are indeed the same size — the illusion occurs at the perceptual level, not the sensory level.

choices.text: Misapplication of size-distance constancy based on depth cues
choices.explanation: The brain interprets the arrow configurations as depth cues (like inside and outside corners of rooms) and applies size-distance scaling — the line that appears 'farther away' is perceived as larger to maintain constancy, even though both lines are equal.

choices.text: Sensory adaptation to arrow-shaped stimuli
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation involves decreased response to constant stimulation — the Müller-Lyer illusion persists regardless of exposure time and is not related to adaptation.

choices.text: Color contrast between the lines and background
choices.explanation: The Müller-Lyer illusion occurs regardless of color — it is driven by the geometry of the arrowheads, not color contrast.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A musician who has spent 20 years playing violin can identify subtle differences in pitch that an untrained listener cannot detect. Both individuals have normal hearing and equivalent auditory thresholds.

questions.question: The musician's superior pitch discrimination is best explained by:

choices.text: A lower absolute threshold for sound
choices.explanation: The passage states both have equivalent auditory thresholds — the musician's advantage is not in detecting quieter sounds but in distinguishing between similar pitches.

choices.text: Enhanced top-down processing from years of musical training
choices.explanation: Years of musical training create extensive knowledge schemas that enhance top-down processing — the musician's brain is better at categorizing, comparing, and distinguishing pitch differences, even though the raw sensory input is similar.

choices.text: More rods and cones in the musician's retina
choices.explanation: Rods and cones are visual receptors — they have no role in pitch discrimination, which is an auditory skill.

choices.text: A larger difference threshold due to daily sound exposure
choices.explanation: A larger difference threshold would mean needing bigger differences to notice — the musician actually has a smaller (more sensitive) difference threshold for pitch.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The visual cliff experiment by Gibson and Walk demonstrated that:

choices.text: Depth perception is entirely learned through experience
choices.explanation: The study found that crawling-age infants already had depth perception, suggesting an innate component — it did not conclude depth perception is entirely learned.

choices.text: Depth perception develops before infants begin to crawl, suggesting an innate component
choices.explanation: Most crawling infants (6-14 months) refused to cross the visual cliff's 'deep' side, indicating they could perceive depth before extensive locomotor experience — suggesting depth perception has an innate or very early developmental basis.

choices.text: Infants prefer deep surfaces over shallow ones
choices.explanation: Infants consistently avoided the deep side — they preferred the shallow side, indicating they perceived and were wary of the apparent drop.

choices.text: Only binocular depth cues are available to infants
choices.explanation: Infants use both monocular and binocular depth cues — the visual cliff can be detected using monocular cues like texture gradient and motion parallax.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Based on the table, which two senses share the same receptor type, and what does this suggest about their interaction?

choices.text: Vision and hearing share mechanoreceptors, so they often produce synesthesia
choices.explanation: Vision uses photoreceptors and hearing uses hair cells — they do not share the same receptor type, and synesthesia is not caused by shared receptors.

choices.text: Taste and smell share chemoreceptors, which explains why they strongly influence each other
choices.explanation: Both taste and smell use chemoreceptors (detecting chemical molecules), which explains their close functional interaction — flavor perception combines both senses, and a blocked nose significantly reduces the ability to taste food.

choices.text: Touch and hearing share hair cells, explaining why loud sounds are felt physically
choices.explanation: Touch uses mechanoreceptors, not hair cells — while very loud sounds can be felt as vibration, this is due to mechanical energy, not shared receptor types.

choices.text: Vision and touch share photoreceptors, which is why blind people have enhanced touch
choices.explanation: Vision uses photoreceptors and touch uses mechanoreceptors — they do not share receptors. Enhanced touch in blind individuals is due to neuroplasticity, not shared receptor types.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Selective attention research demonstrates that:

choices.text: We can fully process all incoming sensory information simultaneously
choices.explanation: Research consistently shows we cannot fully process all sensory input — we must select what to attend to, missing unattended information.

choices.text: We filter sensory information, processing attended stimuli more deeply than unattended ones
choices.explanation: Selective attention research (e.g., dichotic listening, cocktail party effect) shows that we filter and prioritize information — attended stimuli receive deep processing while unattended stimuli are processed only superficially or not at all.

choices.text: Attention has no effect on perception because sensation is automatic
choices.explanation: While sensation is largely automatic, perception is strongly influenced by attention — phenomena like inattentional blindness prove that unattended stimuli can go entirely unperceived.

choices.text: Peripheral vision processes information more accurately than foveal vision
choices.explanation: Foveal vision (central) is far more detailed than peripheral vision — selective attention often directs foveal focus to important stimuli.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The cocktail party effect — the ability to detect your own name in an unattended conversation — provides evidence that:

choices.text: Unattended information receives some level of unconscious processing
choices.explanation: The cocktail party effect shows that unattended auditory streams are not completely blocked — personally relevant information (like one's name) can break through the attentional filter, indicating some unconscious monitoring of unattended channels.

choices.text: All auditory information is fully processed regardless of attention
choices.explanation: If all information were fully processed, people would remember everything said in unattended conversations — the cocktail party effect shows only highly salient stimuli break through.

choices.text: Hearing is more sensitive than vision
choices.explanation: The cocktail party effect demonstrates attentional filtering, not a comparison between hearing and vision sensitivity.

choices.text: Signal detection theory applies only to auditory stimuli
choices.explanation: Signal detection theory applies to all senses — the cocktail party effect illustrates attentional selection, not a limitation of signal detection theory.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A patient with damage to the ventral stream (the 'what' pathway) of the visual cortex can reach out and accurately grasp objects but cannot identify or name them.

questions.question: This dissociation between object recognition and visuomotor control supports the theory that:

choices.text: All visual processing occurs in a single unified system
choices.explanation: The dissociation directly contradicts a single-system model — the patient shows intact visuomotor processing with impaired object recognition.

choices.text: Visual processing is divided into separate pathways for identification and spatial action
choices.explanation: The ventral stream ('what' pathway) processes object identity, while the dorsal stream ('where/how' pathway) processes spatial location and guides action — this patient's damage to the ventral stream impairs recognition but not reaching/grasping.

choices.text: Object recognition depends entirely on touch, not vision
choices.explanation: The patient's grasping ability is visual-motor (dorsal stream) — object recognition is normally a visual process (ventral stream) that is specifically impaired here.

choices.text: The retina processes object identity before sending information to the cortex
choices.explanation: Object identification is a cortical function — the retina transduces light into neural signals but does not identify objects.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Research on cross-cultural differences in visual illusion susceptibility suggests that:

choices.text: All visual illusions affect all humans equally regardless of culture
choices.explanation: Research by Segall, Campbell, and Herskovits found significant cross-cultural differences in illusion susceptibility — people from different visual environments are affected differently.

choices.text: Perceptual processes are partly shaped by the visual environment in which a person develops
choices.explanation: People from 'carpentered' environments (with many right angles and straight lines) are more susceptible to the Müller-Lyer illusion than people from non-carpentered environments, demonstrating that experience shapes perceptual processing.

choices.text: Visual illusions are caused by retinal defects specific to certain populations
choices.explanation: Cross-cultural differences in illusion susceptibility are due to experiential and environmental factors, not retinal differences between populations.

choices.text: Bottom-up processing is identical across all cultures
choices.explanation: While basic sensory processes are similar, the finding that culture affects illusion susceptibility shows that even seemingly 'bottom-up' perceptual processes are influenced by experience.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The phi phenomenon — perceiving motion from a rapid sequence of still images — demonstrates that:

choices.text: The retina can detect actual movement between frames
choices.explanation: There is no actual movement between still frames — the retina detects each static image individually, and the perception of motion is constructed by the brain.

choices.text: The brain constructs the perception of motion from sequential static stimuli
choices.explanation: The phi phenomenon (basis for movies and animation) shows that perception is an active constructive process — the brain creates the experience of smooth motion from a series of rapidly presented still images.

choices.text: Motion perception requires binocular depth cues
choices.explanation: The phi phenomenon can be perceived with one eye — it does not require binocular cues.

choices.text: Only the occipital lobe is involved in motion perception
choices.explanation: While the occipital lobe processes visual information, motion perception also involves area MT/V5 and other cortical areas — the phi phenomenon demonstrates the brain's constructive nature, not a single-lobe process.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: According to the volley principle, neurons in the auditory nerve:

choices.text: Each fire at the exact frequency of any sound wave
choices.explanation: Individual neurons cannot fire faster than about 1,000 times per second — the volley principle addresses how groups of neurons work together to handle higher frequencies.

choices.text: Take turns firing in rapid succession to represent frequencies higher than any single neuron can match
choices.explanation: The volley principle bridges the gap between frequency theory (limited to ~1,000 Hz) and place theory (for high frequencies) — teams of neurons alternate firing so that their combined rate matches mid-range sound frequencies (1,000-4,000 Hz).

choices.text: All fire simultaneously to amplify the signal
choices.explanation: Simultaneous firing would not encode frequency information — the volley principle's key feature is alternating, sequential firing by groups of neurons.

choices.text: Only respond to sound frequencies above 20,000 Hz
choices.explanation: Humans cannot hear above about 20,000 Hz — the volley principle explains mid-range frequency encoding (~1,000-4,000 Hz), not ultrasonic frequencies.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A researcher presents a light stimulus at various intensities to a participant in a dark room. On some trials, no light is presented. The participant must report whether they saw a light. Results show that the participant occasionally reports seeing light when none was presented (false alarm) and occasionally fails to report the light when it was presented (miss).

questions.question: These results are best analyzed using:

choices.text: Weber's Law to calculate the difference threshold
choices.explanation: Weber's Law applies to detecting differences between two stimuli — this experiment involves detecting the presence or absence of a single stimulus, which is a signal detection task.

choices.text: Signal detection theory to separate sensitivity from response bias
choices.explanation: Signal detection theory can analyze hits, misses, false alarms, and correct rejections to separately measure the participant's perceptual sensitivity (d') and their response criterion (beta) — no other framework handles both false alarms and misses.

choices.text: The trichromatic theory to determine which cones responded
choices.explanation: Trichromatic theory explains color processing — this experiment is about detecting light presence, not color discrimination.

choices.text: Classical threshold theory to find the exact detection boundary
choices.explanation: Classical threshold theory assumes a fixed boundary between detection and non-detection — the existence of false alarms and misses violates this assumption and requires signal detection theory.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Change blindness — failing to detect large changes in a visual scene between views — suggests that:

choices.text: Our detailed internal representation of a visual scene is more limited than we assume
choices.explanation: Change blindness reveals that we do not maintain a complete, detailed mental representation of our visual surroundings — we notice far less than we think, and our rich visual experience is partly an illusion constructed by the brain.

choices.text: The retina cannot detect large visual changes
choices.explanation: The retina can detect the changes if attention is directed to them — change blindness is a failure of attention and representation, not retinal detection.

choices.text: Visual memory lasts only a few milliseconds
choices.explanation: While iconic memory is brief, change blindness occurs even with longer viewing times — the issue is that detailed representations are not maintained between views.

choices.text: Bottom-up processing always overrides top-down processing
choices.explanation: Change blindness actually shows the opposite — top-down expectations of stability can override bottom-up detection of actual changes.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Phantom limb pain — the experience of pain in an amputated limb — is most challenging for which theory of pain perception?

choices.text: Any theory requiring physical stimulation of pain receptors
choices.explanation: Phantom limb pain occurs without any physical stimulation (the limb is gone), which challenges purely bottom-up models that require nociceptor activation — the brain itself generates the pain experience, demonstrating that pain is a central nervous system construction.

choices.text: Gate-control theory
choices.explanation: Gate-control theory can partially account for phantom limb pain because it includes central (brain) mechanisms that modulate pain — the theory allows for pain without peripheral input.

choices.text: Top-down processing models
choices.explanation: Top-down models would predict that the brain could generate perceptual experiences — phantom limb pain actually supports the role of central (top-down) processing in pain experience.

choices.text: Signal detection theory
choices.explanation: Signal detection theory addresses how we detect stimuli amid noise — phantom limb pain involves the brain generating a pain signal without any stimulus, which is a different phenomenon.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Feature detection cells in the visual cortex, discovered by Hubel and Wiesel, respond selectively to:

choices.text: Specific colors and brightness levels only
choices.explanation: While some cells respond to color, Hubel and Wiesel's feature detectors specifically respond to edges, lines, and angles at particular orientations — not primarily color.

choices.text: Specific edges, lines, and angles at particular orientations
choices.explanation: Hubel and Wiesel discovered that individual neurons in the visual cortex (simple, complex, and hypercomplex cells) respond to specific visual features like bars of light at particular orientations, moving edges, and angles — building blocks from which the brain constructs object perception.

choices.text: Complete objects like faces and houses
choices.explanation: Feature detectors respond to basic visual elements, not whole objects — higher-level processing areas (like the fusiform face area) integrate features into object recognition.

choices.text: The overall brightness of the entire visual field
choices.explanation: Feature detectors respond to specific localized stimuli, not the overall brightness of the visual field — retinal ganglion cells handle overall light level information.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Synesthesia, in which stimulation of one sense triggers an experience in another (such as 'seeing' colors when hearing music), provides evidence that:

choices.text: Sensory systems are more interconnected in the brain than typically assumed
choices.explanation: Synesthesia suggests cross-activation between normally separate sensory processing areas — it provides evidence that sensory systems have neural connections that, in most people, remain below the threshold for conscious experience.

choices.text: The five senses all use the same type of receptor
choices.explanation: Different senses use different receptor types (photoreceptors, hair cells, chemoreceptors) — synesthesia involves cross-wiring at the brain level, not shared receptors.

choices.text: Perception is entirely determined by the stimulus properties
choices.explanation: Synesthesia demonstrates the opposite — perception can diverge dramatically from stimulus properties when brain wiring is atypical.

choices.text: All people secretly experience synesthesia but learn to ignore it
choices.explanation: Synesthesia appears to have a neurological basis involving atypical cross-connections — it is not a universal experience that some people learn to suppress.
